summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his frustrations with the proliferation of remote controls in home entertainment setups and his search for a workable universal remote solution. After a failed attempt with a Marantz learning remote in 1998, he discovers the Harmony series which takes an activity-based rather than device-based approach to controlling electronics. He critiques the newer H688 model's redesigned button layout as a usability regression, noting that its mushy rubber buttons and contiguous design make it hard to operate by feel. He recommends the older H680/H676 models which retain the superior button layout of the original while offering identical functionality at lower prices.
key_points: Universal remotes often fail because a single remote nobody can figure out is no better than having multiple remotes
Activity-based design (press 'Watch a Movie' instead of configuring 4 devices) represents a major usability improvement over device-based universal remotes
The Harmony H688's redesigned buttons prioritized aesthetics over usability, making buttons hard to distinguish by feel
Simple usability testing or copying proven designs like the TiVo remote would have prevented obvious design failures
Internally identical products can have vastly different usability based solely on physical button design choices
money_quotes: I hate having a remote collection on the coffee table, but a single remote nobody can figure out is hardly any better.
Buttons may be prettier when they're grouped together in one smooth plastic swoosh, but it's nearly impossible to figure out what button you need to press.
A simple round of usability testing would have surely isolated this problem. Or they could have saved some money and just stuck with copying what works – the Tivo remote.
The mushy rubber buttons and contiguous key layout make it very hard to distinguish keys by feel, especially compared to the well-spaced, harder chiclet-style keys of the 659.
They're also cheaper! I browsed some of the threads on the Remote Central Harmony forums and everyone is having generally positive experiences with the harmony series.
key_insight: Good product design requires prioritizing tactile usability over visual aesthetics, and the best solutions often come from copying what already works rather than redesigning for novelty.
